fact: March 21 is often the spring equinox in the Northern Hemisphere, which means day and night are nearly the same length - about 12 hours each!
March 21 is the 80th day of the year (or 81st in leap years).
In Antarctica, March 21 typically marks the beginning of six months of darkness as winter approaches in the Southern Hemisphere.
Tree sap usually starts flowing heavily around this time in northern climates, which is why maple syrup makers get busy tapping trees.
Migrating birds often use the equinox timing as nature's alarm clock - their internal compasses tell them it's time to fly north.
On or near March 21, the sun rises due east and sets due west everywhere on Earth - this only happens around the equinoxes!
In northern regions, snow typically starts melting rapidly around this time, creating puddles and turning dirt roads into muddy soup.
March 21 usually marks when the zodiac sign changes from Pisces to Aries.
Hibernating animals like bears often start waking up around this time as their long winter sleep comes to an end.
Ocean tides can be extra strong around March 21 because the sun and moon's gravitational pull aligns more directly with Earth during the equinox.
In many temperate regions, this is when you first start hearing frogs croaking at night as they wake up from winter.
